channel.title: Error-Handling on cekrem.github.io
channel.description: Recent content in Error-Handling on cekrem.github.io

item.title: Effect Without Effect-TS: Algebraic Thinking in Plain TypeScript
item.description:
A lot of people are reaching for Effect-TS.

Every few months you’ll be three functions deep in some TypeScript service, squinting at a try/catch that swallows four different kinds of failure into one catch (err), and you’ll think: “Effect would fix this.” Then you look at the API surface, the generator syntax, the layers and services and fibers, and you quietly close the tab. Not because it’s bad (not at all; it’s genuinely impressive software.) But it requires some investment, and you’re just not quite sure…

item.title: Kotlin's Rich Errors: Native, Typed Errors Without Exceptions
item.description:
At KotlinConf 2025, the Kotlin team showcased progress toward Rich Errors with union types. After years of watching languages slowly adopt patterns that Elm has championed since day one, it’s exciting to see Kotlin taking this significant step toward more explicit, type-safe error handling. And in a very “native” Kotlin way at that!

I vividly remember this announcement giving my functional heart a pleasant jolt, but I haven’t found the time for a write-up until now. Better late than never, though:
